Solar Energy Adoption Analysis in Australia for ARENA¶

  • Prepared by: Lai Kuen Tsang, Applied Data Analysis Skill Set Course at TAFE
  • Date: 20th April, 2026
  • Confidentiality: Internal (Assignment)

Executive summary¶

This report analyses solar installation adoption trends across Australian states using installation, population, and postcode mapping datasets. The objective was to identify growth patterns, regional differences, and per-capita adoption rates to better understand Australia's solar adoption landscape.

The analysis found that solar installations have grown significantly over time, particularly after 2017, indicating increasing adoption of renewable energy technologies. States such as New South Wales and Queensland recorded the highest number of total installations, driven largely by population size and favourable solar conditions.

When adjusting for population, per-capita adoption revealed different insights, with some smaller states demonstrating relatively higher adoption rates compared to larger states. This suggests that regional policies, incentives, and environmental factors may influence adoption beyond population size alone.

Year-on-year growth analysis also showed periods of accelerated growth, likely influenced by government incentives, declining solar installation costs, and increased awareness of renewable energy benefits.

Following 2020, the growth trend begins to decline, with growth turning negative in 2022. This downturn may be partially attributed to the COVID-19 pandemic, which likely affected installation capacity, labour availability, supply chain disruptions, and overall market activity. In 2023, the growth rate rebounds to positive territory, indicating a recovery phase as market conditions stabilised and installation activity resumed. However, more recent data indicates moderating growth, suggesting the solar market in some regions may be reaching maturity.

Key findings from this analysis include:

  • Strong long-term growth in solar installations across Australia
  • Queensland and New South Wales leading total installations
  • Variation in per-capita adoption across states
  • Evidence of market maturity in recent years
  • Potential growth opportunities in lower-adoption regions

These insights provide valuable information for policy makers, energy providers, and businesses to better understand solar adoption trends and identify future opportunities for renewable energy expansion.

Overall, the analysis demonstrates that solar adoption in Australia continues to grow, with regional differences presenting both challenges and opportunities for future development.

Introduction¶

  • Objectives:

The objective of this analysis is to examine solar installation trends across Australian states, identify growth patterns, and evaluate adoption rates over time. The analysis aims to assess market performance using key metrics such as total installations, capacity growth, and population-normalised adoption rates.

  • Approach:

The analysis was conducted using historical solar installation data combined with population data. The dataset was cleaned, merged, and aggregated by state, year, and quarter. Additional features including year, quarter, and per-capita installation rates were derived to support trend analysis. Key performance indicators including total installations, quarter-on-quarter growth, and year-on-year growth were calculated to evaluate performance.

Methodology¶

Data sources:¶

There are data sources we gather from:

sources = { 'CER_CAP': '../data/sgu-solar-capacity-2011-to-present-and-totals.csv', 'CER_INS': '../data/sgu-solar-installations-2011-to-present-and-totals.csv',

#Two extra data sources:
'ABS': 'https://www.abs.gov.au/statistics/people/population/national-state-and-territory-population/latest-release#states-and-territories' : Population_by_State 2011_2025.csv

'AU_map postcode to states': 'https://github.com/matthewproctor/australianpostcodes/blob/master/australian_postcodes.csv

}

Data Extraction¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd

Load ARENA and ABS datasets

  1. Installation data from ARNEA: df_ins = pd.read_csv(../sgu-solar-installations-2011-to-present-and-totals.csv)

  2. Capacity(kw) data from ARNEA: df_cap = pd.read_csv(../sgu-solar-capacity-2011-to-present-and-totals.csv)

  3. Mapping data of postcode and State: df_pc = pd.read_csv(../australian_postcodes.csv)

  4. Mapping data of state and population: df_pop = pd.read_csv(../Population_by_State 2011_2025.csv)

Analystical Tools¶

The following analytical tools and techniques were used in this analysis reports:

Tools:¶

  • Python
  • Pandas for data manipulation and aggregation
  • Numpy for Numerical computation
  • Matplotlib for data visualization

Analytical Techniques:¶

  • Data cleaning and preprocessing
  • Data merging and tranformation
  • Dervied data vaiables for periods and growth analysis
  • Time-series analysis
  • Per capita analysis
  • Trend and growth rate analysis per quarterly and yearly

Data Collation and Preparation¶

df_pc = pd.read_csv(r'/Users/polly/Downloads/Applied Data Analysis Skill Set/Assessment_2 Due in Apr 5_10/australian_postcodes.csv')

In [ ]:
#remove unwanted columns
df_ins = df_ins.drop(columns=['Historic Total Installation Quantity (2001 - 2010)','Total Installation Quantity'])
In [4]:
#remove space
df_ins.columns = df_ins.columns.str.strip()
In [5]:
#rename the primary key
df_ins.rename(columns={'Small Unit Installation Postcode':'postcode'}, inplace = True)
In [6]:
#set postcode as string and standardlize it
df_ins['postcode']=df_ins['postcode'].astype('string')
df_ins['postcode']=df_ins['postcode'].str.zfill(4)
In [7]:
#unpivot the table
df_ins = pd.melt(df_ins,id_vars='postcode',var_name ='date',value_name='install_qty')
df_ins

In [8]:
# Remove " - Installation Quantity"
df_ins['date'] = df_ins['date'].str.strip().str.replace('- Installation Quantity','',regex = False)
In [11]:
#remove space and empty data
df_ins['date'] = df_ins['date'].astype(str).str.strip()
In [13]:
#remove empty date data
df_ins = df_ins[df_ins['date'] != '']
In [14]:
#set Datetime format
df_ins['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df_ins['date'],format = '%b %Y')
In [16]:
#get year value from date
df_ins['year']= df_ins['date'].dt.year
In [17]:
#get quarter value from date
df_ins['quarter']= df_ins['date'].dt.to_period('Q')
In [18]:
#remove postcode 0000 
df_ins = df_ins[df_ins['postcode'] != '0000'].reset_index(drop=True)
In [19]:
#check the duplicated records
df_ins.duplicated(subset=['postcode','date','year','quarter']).sum()

In [69]:
#set correct data type
df_pop['state'] = df_pop['state'].astype('string').str.strip()
df_pop['population'] = df_pop['population'].astype(int)
In [70]:
# adding state_abbr

state_abbr = {
    'new south wales' : 'NSW',
    'victoria': 'VIC' ,
    'queensland': 'QLD',
    'south australia' :'SA',
    'western australia': 'WA',
    'northern territory':'NT',
    'australian capital territory' : 'ACT',
    'tasmania':'TAS',
    'australia':'AU'
}

df_pop['state_abbr'] = df_pop['state'].map(state_abbr)
In [71]:
df_pop.reset_index(drop = True)

#Use state_abbr as state

df_pop = df_pop.reset_index(drop = True)

df_pop['year'] = df_pop['date'].dt.year

In [81]:
# Add period 2025Q4-2026Q1 to align with install & capacity data with ffill method

df_pop = df_pop.sort_values(['state','quarter'])

fill_all_quarters = pd.period_range(
    df_pop['quarter'].min(),
    df_ins['quarter'].max(),
    freq='Q'
)

# create state + quarter combinations
idx = pd.MultiIndex.from_product(
    [df_pop['state'].unique(), fill_all_quarters],
    names=['state','quarter']
)

#set state and quarter as combo-key and create index 
df_pop_full = (df_pop
    .set_index(['state','quarter'])
    .reindex(idx)   # adding missing rows
    .reset_index()
)

#fill up the data with ffill function
df_pop_full['population'] = (
    df_pop_full.groupby('state')['population'].ffill()
)
In [82]:
#cope with correct date & year value as well
df_pop_full['date'] = df_pop_full['quarter'].dt.to_timestamp()
df_pop_full['year'] = df_pop_full['quarter'].dt.year

#create df to adding missing postcode & state

  • Merge all dataframes to analysis
In [156]:
#make sure one value per state-year
df_pop_clean = (df_pop_full.dropna(subset=['population']).groupby(['state','year'], as_index=False)['population'].median())
In [157]:
#use left join to merge all dfs to prevent data loss
df_merged = (df_ins
    .merge(df_cap[['postcode','date','quarter','year','capacity_kw']],on = ['postcode','date','quarter','year'],how = 'left')
    .merge(df_pc_amended[['postcode','state']],on = 'postcode', how = 'left')
    .merge(df_pop_clean,on = ['state','year'],how = 'left')
)

Data Cleaning and Handling Samples¶

  • Cleaning Columns¶

In [ ]:
#remove unwanted columns
df_ins = df_ins.drop(columns=['Historic Total Installation Quantity (2001 - 2010)','Total Installation Quantity'])
df_cap = df_cap.drop(columns=['Historic Total Rated Power Output In kW (2001 - 2010)','Total Rated Power Output In kW'])

#remove space
df_ins.columns = df_ins.columns.str.strip()
df_cap.columns = df_cap.columns.str.strip()

#df_ins['date'] = df_ins['date'].astype(str).str.strip()
#df_ins = df_ins[df_ins['date'] != '']

#rename the primary key
df_ins.rename(columns={'Small Unit Installation Postcode':'postcode'}, inplace = True)
df_cap.rename(columns={'Small Unit Postcode':'postcode'}, inplace = True)

  • Ensuring correct data types¶

In [ ]:
df_cap['date'] = df_cap['date'].str.strip().str.replace(' - Rated Power Output In kW','',regex = False)
df_cap['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df_cap['date'],format = 'mixed')

  • Removed duplicate records¶

In [ ]:
#check the duplicated rows and remove them
df_pop.duplicated(subset=['state','quarter','year','population']).sum()
df_pop = df_pop.drop_duplicates
df_pc = df_pc.groupby(['postcode','state'],as_index=False).first()

  • Removed unrelative records¶

In [ ]:
#remove postcode 0000 
df_cap= df_cap[df_cap['postcode'] != '0000'].reset_index(drop=True)
df_ins= df_ins[df_ins['postcode'] != '0000'].reset_index(drop=True)

# only keep 2011 onward years to merge installation and capacity
df_pop = df_pop[df_pop['year']>='2011'].reset_index(drop=True)

  • Extract the data from the columns¶

In [ ]:
#get year & quarter value from date column
df_cap['year']= df_cap['date'].dt.year
df_cap['quarter']= df_cap['date'].dt.to_period('Q')

#extract the person populatioin from ABS report
df_pop[['est','gender','state']]=df_pop['state'].str.rsplit('_', n=2, expand = True)
df_pop['gender'] =df_pop['gender'].str.strip()
df_pop['state'] =df_pop['state'].str.strip()
df_pop = df_pop[df_pop['gender'].str.strip()=='persons']
df_pop = df_pop[df_pop['state'].str.strip()!='australia'].reset_index(drop=True)

#only keep "state" column
df_pop = df_pop.drop(columns = ['est','gender'])
df_pop['state'] = df_pop['state'].astype('string').str.strip()

  • Handling Missing Values¶

In [ ]:
#create pd df to add missing postcode & state 

missing_pc = pd.DataFrame(
    {'postcode': ['1848', '2552', '4062', '4063', '4237', '4276', '5175', 
                  '5207', '5312', '5875', '6013', '6643', '7802', '8576', '9729'
                ],
      'state': ['NSW','NSW','QLD','QLD','QLD','QLD','SA','SA',
                'SA','SA','WA','WA','TAS','NT','QLD'
                ]
     })

# join the missing postcode to df_pc
df_pc_amended = pd.concat([df_pc,missing_pc],ignore_index = True)

  • Unpivoting data with melt function¶

In [ ]:
#unpivot the table
df_ins = pd.melt(df_ins,id_vars='postcode',var_name ='date',value_name='install_qty')
df_cap = pd.melt(df_cap,id_vars='postcode',var_name ='date',value_name='capacity_kw')
df_pop = pd.melt(df_pop,id_vars = ['date'],var_name = 'state', value_name = 'population')

Analysis and Findings¶

Section 1 - Overview¶

Total Installation Over Time:

In [183]:
total_install = df_merged['install_qty'].sum()
print(f'Total Installations:, {total_install:,}')

Total Installations:, 4,027,387

Total Capacity Installed Over Time:

In [184]:
total_capacity = df_merged['capacity_kw'].sum()
print(f'Total Capacity(KW):, {total_capacity:,}')

Total Capacity(KW):, 27,823,546.32

Lastest Quarter Installations:

Note: Since the dataset only includes data up to January 2026, the 2026 Q1 period is incomplete. Therefore, this report considers 2025 Q4 as the most recent complete quarter.

In [ ]:
#latest_quarter = df_merged['quarter'].max()
#remove incomplete quarter 2026Q1
latest_full_quarter = latest_quarter - 1
latest_install = df_merged[df_merged['quarter'] == latest_full_quarter]['install_qty'].sum()

print(f'Latest Quarter: {str(latest_full_quarter)}')
print(f'Latest Installations: {latest_install:,}')

Growth Rate (Quarter to Quarter):

In [232]:
df_quarter = (df_merged[df_merged['quarter']!= "2026Q1"].groupby('quarter')['install_qty'].sum().sort_index().reset_index())
df_quarter['QoQ_growth'] = df_quarter['install_qty'].pct_change() * 100
df_quarter['QoQ_growth'] = df_quarter['QoQ_growth'].round(2)
#print(df_quarter)

Growth Rate (Year to Year):

In [233]:
# Exclude 2026 data as it is incompleted quarter period data
df_year = (df_merged[df_merged['year'] != 2026].groupby('year')['install_qty'].sum().reset_index())
df_year['YoY_growth'] = df_year['install_qty'].pct_change() * 100
df_year'YoY_growth'] = df_year['YoY_growth'].round(2)
#print(df_year)

Section 2 — Adoption Trends¶

Solar Installations Trend (Quarterly) : Quarterly Installations Over Time

In [226]:
df_quarter = (df_merged[df_merged['quarter']!= "2026Q1"].groupby('quarter')['install_qty'].sum().sort_index().reset_index())
df_quarter['QoQ_growth'] = df_quarter['install_qty'].pct_change() * 100
df_quarter['QoQ_growth'] = df_quarter['QoQ_growth'].round(2)
#print(df_quarter)
In [196]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

#calculate the quarterly installtion ove time but exclude 2026Q1 as imcompleted quarter data
df_quarter_ins = (df_merged[df_merged['quarter']!= "2026Q1"].groupby('quarter')['install_qty'].sum().sort_index().reset_index())

#plot the data to line chart

plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.plot(df_quarter_ins['quarter'].dt.to_timestamp(), df_quarter_ins['install_qty'], marker='o')
plt.title('Solar Installations Trend (Quarterly)')
plt.xlabel('Quarter')
plt.ylabel('Installations')
plt.gca().yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    ticker.StrMethodFormatter('{x:,.0f}')
)

plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.xticks(
    df_quarter_ins['quarter'].dt.to_timestamp()[::4],
    df_quarter_ins['quarter'].astype(str)[::4],
    rotation=45)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Capacity Growth Trend : Total Capacity Installed Over Time

In [197]:
# calculate the Total Capacity Installed Over Time
df_quarter_cap = (df_merged[df_merged['quarter']!= "2026Q1"].groupby('quarter')['capacity_kw'].sum().sort_index().reset_index())

#Plot the data to line chart
plt.figure(figsize=(10,5))
plt.plot(df_quarter_cap['quarter'].dt.to_timestamp(), df_quarter_cap['capacity_kw'], marker='o')
plt.title('Total Capacity Installed Over Time (Quarterly)')
plt.xlabel('Quarterly')
plt.ylabel('Capacity_kw')
plt.gca().yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    ticker.StrMethodFormatter('{x:,.0f}')
)
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.xticks(
    df_quarter_cap['quarter'].dt.to_timestamp()[::4],
    df_quarter_cap['quarter'].astype(str)[::4],
    rotation=45
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The insights from solar installation and capacity installed over the time:

  • solar adoption increased significantly after 2017
  • The growth was being accelerated during recent years.

Combine installation and capacity trend

Note:The data has been normalized for visualization purposes, as installation and capacity are measured on different scales.

In [200]:
#plot the Installation vs Capacity Collrelation View

ins = df_quarter_ins.copy()
cap = df_quarter_cap.copy()

ins['install_norm'] = ins['install_qty'] / ins['install_qty'].max()
cap['capacity_norm'] = cap['capacity_kw'] / cap['capacity_kw'].max()

plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))

plt.plot(ins['quarter'].astype(str), ins['install_norm'], label='Installations (normalised)', marker='o')
plt.plot(cap['quarter'].astype(str), cap['capacity_norm'], label='Capacity (normalised)', marker='o')

plt.title('Normalised Solar Installations vs Capacity (Correlation View)')
plt.xlabel('Quarter')
plt.ylabel('Normalised Value (0–1)')
plt.xticks(ins['quarter'].astype(str)[::4], rotation=45)
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • Overall Trend (Installations vs Capacity) insights:

Both solar installations and installed capacity show a strong upward trend over time, indicating continuous adoption of rooftop solar systems. Even if installation growth slows, total energy generation potential can still rise strongly. However, installation growth and capacity growth do not always move at the same speed, suggesting changes in system size over time.

Section 3 — Geographic Insights¶

State Comparison Dashboard :Installations by State

In [201]:
#compare the state installation
df_state = (df_merged.groupby('state')['install_qty'].sum().sort_values(ascending = False).reset_index())

# plot the data to bar chart
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.bar(df_state['state'], df_state['install_qty'])
plt.title('Solar Installations by State')
plt.xlabel('State')
plt.ylabel('Installations (Qty)')
plt.gca().yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    ticker.StrMethodFormatter('{x:,.0f}')
)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The history saving thread hit an unexpected error (OperationalError('attempt to write a readonly database')).History will not be written to the database.

Top 10 Solar Postcodes:

In [227]:
# group top 10 postcode with state
df_top_pc = (
    df_merged.groupby(['postcode', 'state'])['install_qty']
    .sum()
    .sort_values(ascending=False)
    .head(10)
    .reset_index()
)

# create combined label
df_top_pc['postcode_state'] = (
    df_top_pc['postcode'].astype(str) + ' (' + df_top_pc['state'] + ')'
)

# plot
plt.figure(figsize=(9,6))

plt.barh(
    df_top_pc['postcode_state'],
    df_top_pc['install_qty']
)

plt.title('Top 10 Solar Postcodes')
plt.xlabel('Installations (Qty)')
plt.ylabel('Postcode (State)')

plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(
    ticker.StrMethodFormatter('{x:,.0f}')
)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Section 4 — Solar Adoption Rate Per Capita Analysis¶

  • Solar Adoption Rate by State 2011-2025 (per capita)
In [215]:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as ticker

df_install_state = (df_merged.groupby('state')['install_qty'].sum().reset_index())
df_pop_state = (df_merged.groupby('state')['population'].mean().reset_index())

df_per_cap = df_install_state.merge(df_pop_state, on='state')
df_per_cap['install_per_capita'] = (df_per_cap['install_qty'] / df_per_cap['population'])
df_per_cap = df_per_cap.sort_values('install_per_capita', ascending=True)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

bars = plt.barh(df_per_cap['state'],df_per_cap['install_per_capita'])

plt.title('Solar Adoption Rate by State (2011-2025)')
plt.xlabel('Adoption Rate (%)')
plt.ylabel('State')

# Format as percentage
plt.gca().xaxis.set_major_formatter(ticker.PercentFormatter(1))

# Add labels
for bar in bars:
    width = bar.get_width()
    plt.text(
        width + 0.002,
        bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
        f'{width:.1%}',
        va='center'
    )

plt.xlim(0, df_per_cap['install_per_capita'].max()*1.1)

plt.grid(axis='x', linestyle='--', alpha=0.4)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • Solar Adoption Rate by State Insights:

South Australia leads solar adoption rate(installation per capita) at 23.6%, followed by Queensland and Western Australia, while Tasmania and Northern Territory show the lowest uptake, highlighting strong regional differences influenced by sunlight, housing type, and policy incentives.

Section 5 — Year-on-Year Growth¶

In [206]:
#Year on Year Growth
df_year = (df_merged.groupby('year')['install_qty'].sum().reset_index())
df_year = df_year[df_year['year'] < 2026] # exclude 2026 incompleted quarter data
df_year['growth'] = df_year['install_qty'].pct_change() * 100

# plot the year-on-year trend

plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))
plt.plot(df_year['year'], df_year['growth'])
plt.grid(True, axis = 'y',linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
plt.title('Year-on-Year Growth Rate')
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Y-o-Y Growth %')
plt.xticks(df_year['year'],rotation = 40) 
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
  • Year-On-Year Chart Insights:

Between 2016 and 2017, there is a strong spike in growth, indicating rapid adoption of solar installations. From 2017 to 2020, growth remains consistently positive, suggesting strong market expansion during this period. From 2020 onwards, there is a noticeable decline, which may be partly influenced by the COVID-19 pandemic, affecting supply chains, workforce availability, and installation activities. Growth becomes negative in 2022, before recovering in 2023, indicating a temporary slowdown followed by market recovery. However, from 2023 to 2025, growth declines again with policy changed, some states changed direct rebate policy which contribute the growth drop trend, such as QLD, VIC, SA & TAS. However, year-on-year growth demonstrates a consistent long-term increase in solar adoption, indicating sustained market expansion.

Discussion¶

The analysis shows clear differences in rooftop solar adoption across Australian states (2011–2025), driven by geography, housing type, and policy support.

South Australia leads significantly with 23.6% adoption, reflecting strong incentives and high energy costs. Queensland (21.6%) and Western Australia (19.6%) also show strong uptake due to favourable sunlight conditions and higher rates of detached housing.

New South Wales (13.2%) and Victoria (12.8%) show moderate adoption despite large populations, likely due to urban density and more apartment living. Tasmania (11.2%) and Northern Territory (9.7%) record the lowest adoption, influenced by environmental and infrastructure limitations.

Overall, solar adoption varies widely across states, with stronger performance in sunnier and less urbanised regions.

Recommendations¶

1. Target NSW and VIC for adoption growth¶

Since NSW and VIC represent large populations but relatively lower adoption rates, we can making them key growth areas. Improve uptake through higher rebates, faster approvals, and support for apartment solar solutions. My recommended Policy interventions such as:

  • Increased rebates for rooftop solar
  • Streamlined approval processes for installations
  • Incentives for apartment solar solutions

Those processes could significantly improve uptake.

2. Maintain and expand incentives in high-performing states (SA, QLD, WA)¶

South Australia, Queensland, and Western Australia already show strong adoption. However, continued support is important to:

  • Sustain momentum
  • Encourage battery storage adoption
  • Prevent market saturation slowdown

We can focus shift from adoption to energy storage and system optimisation.

3. Develop tailored strategies for low-adoption regions (TAS, NT)¶

Tasmania and Northern Territory require region-specific strategies, such as:

  • Community solar projects (shared solar farms)
  • Government-supported installations
  • Off-grid or hybrid energy systems in remote areas

These regions may not scale through rooftop solar alone due to environmental and demographic constraints.

4. Promote apartment and urban solar solutions¶

Lower adoption in NSW and VIC highlights the need for:

  • Shared solar schemes (virtual net metering)
  • Building-integrated solar systems
  • Community energy models

This addresses structural barriers in high-density urban environments.

5. Enhance data-driven monitoring of solar adoption¶

To improve future decision-making:

  1. Track adoption at quarterly/state level consistently
  2. Standardise population adjustments (per capita metrics)
  3. Incorporate weather, income, and housing data for deeper insights

This will enable more precise policy targeting and forecasting.

References¶

  • Clean Energy Regulator, Australian Government (2026), Quarterly Carbon Market Report September Quarter 2022, https://cer.gov.au/markets/reports-and-data/quarterly-carbon-market-reports/quarterly-carbon-market-report-september-quarter-2022/small-scale-technology-certificates-stcs

  • Department of Climate Change, Energy, the Environment and Water (2024), Solar rebates and incentives, Australian Government, viewed 18 April 2026, https://www.energy.gov.au
